# Research: Non-Code Work Estimation (Testing, Builds, Releases, Docs)
<!-- Generated By: Claude Code (claude-opus-4-6) -->

## Research Date: 2026-03-20

---

## SOURCE 1: IDC Report — "How Do Software Developers Spend Their Time?"

- **URL**: https://www.infoworld.com/article/3831759/developers-spend-most-of-their-time-not-coding-idc-report.html
- **Original report**: https://my.idc.com/getdoc.jsp?containerId=US53204725
- **Author**: Adam Resnick (IDC Research Manager for Modern Software)
- **Publication**: February 2025 (based on 2024 survey data)
- **Reported by**: Paul Krill, InfoWorld

### Key Data Points
- Application development accounted for only **16% of developers' time** in 2024, up from 15% in 2023
- The majority of time was spent on **operational and supportive tasks** (84%)
- Largest time consumers: implementing CI/CD processes, application performance monitoring, infrastructure performance monitoring
- **Security time rose from 8% to 13%** (2023 to 2024) — the largest year-over-year shift

### Methodology
- Survey question: "Thinking over the typical month, what percent of your time is spent on the following tasks/activities?"
- Respondents chose from a task list including developing applications, security, deploying code, implementing CI/CD

### Caveats
- The 16% figure represents "application development" broadly, not just coding — the definition may be narrower than other surveys
- Full report behind IDC paywall; only summary data available via InfoWorld reporting

## SOURCE 2: Tidelift/SonarSource Developer Survey

- **URL**: https://www.sonarsource.com/blog/how-much-time-do-developers-spend-actually-writing-code/
- **URL**: https://blog.tidelift.com/how-much-time-do-developers-spend-actually-writing-code
- **URL**: https://thenewstack.io/how-much-time-do-developers-spend-actually-writing-code/
- **Organization**: Tidelift (now part of Sonar)
- **Publication**: Referenced across multiple outlets

### Key Data Points — Developer Time Allocation
| Activity | All Respondents | Software Developers Specifically |
|----------|----------------|----------------------------------|
| Writing new code / improving existing code | 32% | 39% |
| Code maintenance | 19% | 22% |
| Testing | 12% | — |
| Responding to security issues | 4% | — |
| Meetings and management/operational tasks | 23% | 14% |
| **Total managing code** | **35%** | — |

- In organizations with **500+ developers**, maintenance rises to **32%** of time
- DevOps engineers and managers spend **34%** of their time in meetings

### Direct Quotes
- "Respondents spend less than one-third of their time writing new code or improving existing code (32%)"
- "Respondents spend 35% of their time managing code, including code maintenance (19%), testing (12%) and responding to security issues (4%)"

### Caveats
- Tidelift was acquired by Sonar; the survey is referenced across multiple sites with slightly different framing
- Self-reported data subject to recall bias

## SOURCE 5: Testing Effort as Percentage of SDLC

- **URL**: https://hypersense-software.com/blog/2025/07/19/software-development-effort-allocation-dev-qa-design-pm-ratio/
- **URL**: https://zhiminzhan.medium.com/70-of-sdlc-effort-is-on-software-testing-or-related-activities-2a04ca5bef6d
- **URL**: https://betterembsw.blogspot.com/2015/02/tester-to-developer-ratio-should-be-11.html

### Key Data Points — Testing Effort by Project Type
| Project Type | QA/Testing as % of Total Effort |
|---|---|
| Low criticality / small projects (prototypes, simple web apps) | 10-20% |
| Typical commercial applications | 20-40% |
| Regulated / safety-critical (medical, aerospace, finance) | 40-60%+ |
| Mature products in maintenance mode | 15-30% |

### General Rules of Thumb
- Development (coding) typically consumes **40-60%** of effort
- QA/testing follows at **20-30%**
- Design/UI and project management each **10-20%**
- Common baseline: take **40% of development effort** as estimated testing effort
- Studies indicate **40-50%** of software development cost is devoted to testing
- One observer concluded ~**70%** of all SDLC effort is on testing or related activities

### Embedded Systems Specific
- 33% for development
- 17% for developer V&V (peer reviews, unit test)
- 44% for V&V team integration and system testing
- Only ~1/3 of time on actual development vs verification/validation

### Developer-to-Tester Ratios
- Microsoft: 1:1 tester-to-developer headcount
- Diminishing returns at ~60% testers-to-developers ratio (e.g., 10 devs and 6 testers)

## SOURCE 6: Code Review Time

- **URL**: https://blog.codacy.com/10-facts-about-code-reviews-and-quality
- **URL**: https://blog.codacy.com/impact-of-code-reviews-on-developer-productivity-and-code-quality-a-quantitative-assessment
- **URL**: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/jetbrains_how-much-time-developers-spend-on-code-reviews-activity-7308224032296869890-kEOL

### Key Data Points
- Average code review time: **~5 hours per week** (~12.5% of work week)
- 75% of developers spend up to 5 hours/week on code reviews (Stack Overflow survey)
- Breakdown: 12% spend <1 hr/week, 31% spend 2-3 hrs, 31% spend 4-5 hrs
- Median: ~4-5 hours/week (10-12% of a 40-hour work week)
- Under 10% spend >10 hours weekly on reviews

### Impact Findings
- Spending >1 day/week reviewing does NOT correlate with better perceived code quality
- 45% cite "Lack of Time" as obstacle to reviewing code; 34% cite "Pressure to Ship"
- Reviewed code developers spend 10% more time on new features, 10% less on bug fixes
- Strict reviewers: 24% time on bugs, 54% on features. Lenient reviewers: 31% on bugs, 43% on features

### AI in Code Review
- 81% of developers using AI for code review saw quality improvements (Qodo 2025 report)
- vs. 55% of fast-moving teams without AI review

## SOURCE 7: Agile Sprint Overhead

- **URL**: https://medium.com/serious-scrum/how-long-is-a-developer-day-b5a2e4ab1e1c
- **Author**: Seamus Connolly

### Key Data Points — Scrum Event Time-Boxes (2-Week Sprint)
| Event | Duration |
|---|---|
| Sprint planning | 4 hours |
| Daily scrum | 15 min/day (2.5 hrs total over sprint) |
| Sprint review | 2 hours |
| Sprint retrospective | 1.5 hours |
| Backlog refinement | ≤10% of developer capacity |

- Formal Scrum events alone reduce available production time by **~25% per day** on average
- Typical productive coding/testing time: **5-6 hours per 8-hour day**
- 44.45% of developers consider Daily Stand-Ups ineffective

## SOURCE 8: METR Randomized Controlled Trial — AI and Developer Productivity

- **URL**: https://metr.org/blog/2025-07-10-early-2025-ai-experienced-os-dev-study/
- **ArXiv**: https://arxiv.org/abs/2507.09089
- **Authors**: Joel Becker, Nate Rush, Elizabeth Barnes, David Rein
- **Published**: July 10, 2025
- **Organization**: METR (Model Evaluation and Threat Research)

### Study Design
- **Type**: Randomized Controlled Trial (RCT)
- **Participants**: 16 experienced open-source developers
- **Tasks**: 246 real issues, randomly assigned to allow/disallow AI tools
- **AI tools used**: Cursor Pro with Claude 3.5/3.7 Sonnet
- **Average task duration**: ~2 hours
- **Developer compensation**: $150/hr
- **Developer experience**: Average 5 years and 1,500 commits per repository
- **Repositories**: Average 22k+ stars, 1M+ lines of code

### Key Findings
- **AI made experienced developers 19% SLOWER** (not faster)
- Before tasks: developers forecast AI would reduce time by **24%**
- After tasks: developers estimated AI reduced time by **20%**
- **Actual result**: AI increased completion time by **19%**
- **Perception gap**: 39 percentage points between belief and reality
- 3/4 of participants saw reduced performance; only 1/4 saw improvement

### Direct Quote
"Before starting tasks, developers forecast that allowing AI will reduce completion time by 24%. After completing the study, developers estimate that allowing AI reduced completion time by 20%. Surprisingly, allowing AI actually increases completion time by 19%."

### Explanations
- Developers spent much time cleaning up AI-generated code
- Screen recordings showed more idle time with AI (not just model wait time)
- AI may reduce cognitive effort, making multi-tasking/zoning out easier
- Experienced developers in familiar codebases may be too fast for AI to help

### Caveats
- Small sample (16 developers)
- Specific context: experienced developers in their own mature OSS projects
- Authors acknowledge AI may help more in other contexts (less experienced devs, unfamiliar codebases)
- Snapshot of early-2025 AI capabilities

---

## SOURCE 9: Google DORA 2024 Report — AI Impact on Delivery

- **URL**: https://dora.dev/research/2024/dora-report/
- **URL**: https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/devops-sre/announcing-the-2024-dora-report
- **Summary**: https://www.gitclear.com/research/google_dora_2024_summary_ai_impact
- **Organization**: Google Cloud DORA team
- **Publication**: 2024

### Key Data Points — AI Impact per 25% Increase in AI Adoption
| Metric | Change |
|---|---|
| Delivery throughput | -1.5% (decrease) |
| Delivery stability | **-7.2% (decrease)** |
| Individual productivity | +2.1% (increase) |
| Job satisfaction | +2.6% (increase) |
| Code quality | +3.4% (increase) |
| Code review speed | +3.1% (increase) |
| Documentation quality | **+7.5% (increase)** |

### AI Adoption Stats
- 75.9% of respondents use AI for at least part of their job
- Top 5 AI tasks: code writing, summarizing information, code explanation, code optimization, documentation

### Elite vs. Low Performers
- 127x faster change lead time
- 182x more deployments per year
- 8x lower change failure rate
- 2,293x faster failed deployment recovery

### Direct Quote (paraphrased)
"AI adoption may negatively impact software delivery performance. As AI adoption increased, it was accompanied by an estimated decrease in delivery throughput by 1.5%, and an estimated reduction in delivery stability by 7.2%."

### Trust
- 39% of respondents reported little to no trust in AI-generated code

## SOURCE 10: GitHub Copilot Productivity Studies

- **URL**: https://arxiv.org/abs/2302.06590 (original study)
- **URL**: https://linearb.io/blog/is-github-copilot-worth-it
- **URL**: https://www.harness.io/blog/the-impact-of-github-copilot-on-developer-productivity-a-case-study
- **Authors**: Various (GitHub Research for original RCT)

### Key Data Points
| Metric | Finding |
|---|---|
| Task completion speed (GitHub RCT) | 55.8% faster with Copilot |
| General reported time savings | 30-60% |
| Enterprise reduction in code-related dev time | 33-36% |
| PR increase (real-world) | +10.6% |
| Cycle time reduction | 3.5 hours |
| PR time reduction | 4x (9.6 days to 2.4 days) |

### Duolingo Case Study
- New-to-codebase engineers: **25% speed increase**
- Experienced developers: **10% speed boost**
- Code review turnaround time dropped **67%**

### Adoption Scale (2025)
- 15 million+ developers using Copilot (400% increase in 12 months)
- 41% of all code written in 2025 is AI-generated
- 90% of Fortune 100 companies use Copilot

### Counterpoint Data
- Uplevel: developers with Copilot saw **significantly higher bug rate** with consistent issue throughput
- GitClear: AI-generated code has **41% higher churn rate**
- 46% of developers don't fully trust AI outputs
- Debugging AI code often cancels out speed gains

## SOURCE 11: GitClear — AI Code Quality Analysis

- **URL**: https://www.gitclear.com/coding_on_copilot_data_shows_ais_downward_pressure_on_code_quality
- **URL**: https://www.gitclear.com/ai_assistant_code_quality_2025_research
- **Organization**: GitClear
- **Published**: February 2025 (covering 2020-2024 data)
- **Scale**: 211 million changed lines of code from Google, Microsoft, Meta, and enterprise C-Corps

### Key Data Points
- Code churn: **7.9% of newly added code revised within 2 weeks** (2024) vs. 5.5% (2020) — **41% increase**
- Code churn projected to **double** 2024 vs 2021 pre-AI baseline
- Copy/pasted code: rose from **8.3% (2021) to 12.3% (2024)**
- Code blocks with 5+ duplicated lines increased **8x during 2024 alone**
- Refactoring-associated changes: sunk from **25% (2021) to <10% (2024)**

### Direct Quote
"Fast code-adding is desirable if you're working in isolation or on a Greenfield problem. But hastily added code is caustic to the teams expected to maintain it afterward."

## SOURCE 12: AI-Generated Code Downstream Testing Impact

- **URL**: https://www.heise.de/en/news/AI-accelerates-code-but-delays-tests-11215940.html
- **URL**: https://addyosmani.com/blog/code-review-ai/
- **URL**: https://pie.inc/blog/testing-ai-generated-code/
- **URL**: https://www.getpanto.ai/blog/ai-coding-productivity-statistics

### Key Findings — The Bottleneck Shift
- Testing, debugging, and verification already account for **~50% of time** in conventional development
- AI shifts the bottleneck: generating code becomes faster, but **proving it works remains complex**
- PRs getting ~18% larger with AI adoption
- Incidents per PR up ~24%
- Change failure rates up ~30%

### Alarming Statistics
- 59% of engineering leaders: AI-generated code introduced errors **at least half the time**
- 67% spend more time debugging AI-written code than their own
- 68% spend extra effort fixing security vulnerabilities from AI suggestions
- ~45% of AI-generated code contains security flaws
- 41% of all code now written by machines (256 billion lines in 2024)

### IBM Error Cost Escalation (widely cited)
| Phase | Error Fix Cost Multiplier (vs. Design Phase) |
|---|---|
| Design | 1x |
| Implementation | 6x |
| Testing | 15x |
| Production | 100x |

### Direct Quote
"AI accelerates both value creation and risk creation. Delivery metrics such as lead time, defect rate, and deployment frequency often remain unchanged because bottlenecks migrate downstream to review and validation stages."

---

## SOURCE 13: AI Test Case Generation — Academic Study

- **URL**: https://arxiv.org/html/2412.03693v1
- **Title**: "System Test Case Design from Requirements Specifications: Insights and Challenges of Using ChatGPT"
- **Published**: December 4, 2024 (arXiv)
- **Model used**: ChatGPT-4o Turbo

### Key Data Points
| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Average test cases generated per use case | 10-11 |
| Directly useful test cases | 72.5% |
| Test cases identifying unconsidered edge cases | 15.2% |
| **Total valid test conditions** | **87.7%** |
| Not applicable (irrelevant/changed requirements) | 9.7% |

### Methodology
- Two-stage prompt-chaining approach (superior to single-prompt)
- Stage 1: Familiarize LLM with Software Requirements Specification (SRS)
- Stage 2: Generate test cases per use case
- Evaluated against 5 real software engineering projects
- Developer evaluation of generated test cases

### Limitations
- Presence of redundant test conditions in generated cases
- Quality depends heavily on prompt engineering and domain context provided

---

## SOURCE 14: AI in Software Testing — Rainforest QA 2025 Report

- **URL**: https://www.rainforestqa.com/blog/ai-in-software-testing-report-2025
- **Organization**: Rainforest QA
- **Published**: 2025
- **Sample**: 600+ software developers across US, Canada, UK, Australia

### Key Data Points
- 75% of teams using traditional test automation have adopted AI testing
- 65-70% of organizations remain in **pilot or proof-of-concept** phases (not enterprise-wide)
- 68% actively using GenAI (34%) or have roadmaps after successful pilots (34%)
- 65% say higher productivity is primary quality outcome of AI
- Test execution timelines shortened by as much as **80%**
- Claims of **10x productivity improvements** and **95% maintenance reduction** (from AI-native platforms)
- Gartner 2024: 80% of enterprises will integrate AI-augmented testing by 2027 (up from 15% in early 2023)

### Caveats
- Vendor report (Rainforest QA is a testing vendor — potential bias)
- "10x productivity" and "95% maintenance reduction" claims come from vendor marketing, not peer-reviewed studies
- Most organizations still in pilot phases despite headline adoption numbers

## SOURCE 15: Stack Overflow Developer Survey 2024-2025

- **URL**: https://survey.stackoverflow.co/2024/
- **URL**: https://survey.stackoverflow.co/2024/ai
- **URL**: https://survey.stackoverflow.co/2025/

### Key Data Points — 2024
- 76% using or planning to use AI tools (up from 70% prior year)
- 62% currently using AI tools (up from 44%)
- 82% use AI to write code; 46% of non-users most curious about testing
- 81% say AI increases productivity
- 81% expect AI to be more integrated in documenting code
- 80% expect more AI integration in testing code
- 45% say AI is bad/very bad at handling complex tasks

### Key Data Points — 2025
- Trust in AI accuracy: **33%** (down from 43% in 2024)
- AI favorability: **60%** (down from 72% in 2024)
- 66% cite "almost right but not quite" as top frustration
- 45% say debugging AI code takes more time than expected

## SOURCE 16: Addy Osmani — Code Review in the Age of AI

- **URL**: https://addyosmani.com/blog/code-review-ai/
- **Author**: Addy Osmani (Google Chrome engineering lead)
- **Also at**: https://addyo.substack.com/p/code-review-in-the-age-of-ai

### Key Findings
- PRs ~18% larger with AI adoption; incidents per PR up ~24%; change failure rates up ~30%
- ~45% of AI-generated code contains security flaws
- AI reviewers can catch 70-80% of low-hanging fruit (null pointers, missing test coverage, anti-patterns)
- OCaml maintainers rejected 13,000-line AI-generated PR — reviewing AI code is "more taxing" than human code

### Direct Quote
"When a developer submits AI-generated code they don't fully understand, they're breaking the knowledge transfer mechanism that makes teams resilient."

## SOURCE 17: LLM Documentation Generation

- **URL**: https://medium.com/gft-engineering/evaluating-an-llm-code-documentation-generation-application-719b57f801e5
- **URL**: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1877050924026176
- **URL**: https://arxiv.org/html/2507.19113v1

### Key Findings
- LLMs generate coherent, well-structured documents but **lack detail and adaptability**
- User stories: clear and concise but **incomplete**
- Epics: simplistic outlines lacking full functionality coverage
- Time savings from LLM-drafted requirements: **10-15%** (analyst estimate)
- DORA 2024: 25% increase in AI adoption associated with **7.5% increase in documentation quality**
- LLM-generated docs **require human revision** — advocating for "synergistic approach"

### Databricks Case Study
- Team of 2 engineers, 1 month to build custom documentation LLM
- Total development cost: under $1,000
- Result: 10x reduction in inference cost vs. general SaaS models

## SOURCE 18: CI/CD Pipeline Metrics

- **URL**: https://www.jetbrains.com/teamcity/ci-cd-guide/devops-ci-cd-metrics/
- **URL**: https://www.techtarget.com/searchsoftwarequality/CI-CD-pipelines-explained-Everything-you-need-to-know
- **URL**: https://about.gitlab.com/topics/ci-cd/continuous-integration-metrics/

### Key Data Points
- Well-optimized pipeline: integration in **minutes**, testing cycles in **hours**
- If build takes days, "a great deal of valuable time is being wasted"
- CircleCI study: CI/CD reduces time-to-market by **33%** vs. traditional approaches
- Puppet DevOps survey: CI/CD teams report **60% higher team efficiency**
- Forrester: CI/CD adoption = **50% reduction** in development and operations costs
- CI/CD = **50% reduction** in change failure rate
- CI/CD = **40% reduction** in post-release defects

### DORA Four Key Metrics
1. Delivery Lead Time (code commit to production)
2. Deployment Frequency
3. Time to Restore Service (TTRS)
4. Change Failure Rate (CFR)

### Release Management Benchmarks
- Change Failure Rate target: <15% (good), <10% (elite)
- MTTR target: <1 hour
- Post-release bugs target: <5 per release

## SOURCE 19: Software Development Effort Estimation — General

- **URL**: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Software_development_effort_estimation
- **URL**: https://vadimkravcenko.com/shorts/project-estimates/
- **URL**: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1877050915035681

### Key Data Points
- Mean effort overrun: **~30%** (not decreasing over time)
- Acceptable estimation error margin: **5-10%**
- ~60% of famous failed projects cite cost overrun and delivery delays
- Effort distribution (one study): software development 72.6%, ongoing lifecycle 17.5%, QC/testing 9.9%
- 40% of work effort in code and unit test phase; remaining 60% across other SDLC areas

## SYNTHESIS: Consolidated Time Allocation Data

### Developer Time Allocation — Cross-Source Summary
| Source | Coding | Testing | Code Review | Maintenance | Meetings/Ops | Security | Other |
|--------|--------|---------|-------------|-------------|--------------|----------|-------|
| IDC 2024 | 16% | — | — | — | — | 13% | 71% (ops) |
| Tidelift/Sonar | 32% | 12% | — | 19% | 23% | 4% | 10% |
| Tidelift (devs only) | 39% | — | — | 22% | 14% | — | 25% |
| Ali Dasdan | 41% | — | significant | significant | — | — | — |
| Agile Sprint overhead | — | — | — | — | ~25% (ceremonies) | — | — |
| Code Review surveys | — | — | 10-12.5% | — | — | — | — |

### Non-Code Work: What AI Can and Cannot Accelerate

#### AI CAN Accelerate (with caveats)
| Task | AI Acceleration Evidence | Caveats |
|------|------------------------|---------|
| Test case generation | 87.7% valid test cases (ChatGPT study); up to 80% shorter execution timelines | Quality depends on prompt engineering; most orgs still in pilot phase |
| Documentation drafting | 7.5% quality improvement per 25% AI adoption (DORA); 10-15% time savings | Outputs lack detail; require human revision |
| Code review assistance | 81% saw quality improvements (Qodo); AI catches 70-80% of low-hanging fruit | Cannot replace architectural/business logic review |
| Boilerplate/repetitive code | 55% faster task completion (GitHub RCT) | 41% higher churn rate; higher bug rates |
| Code summarization/explanation | Top 5 AI task (DORA 2024) | — |

#### AI CANNOT Meaningfully Accelerate (current evidence)
| Task | Why AI Falls Short |
|------|-------------------|
| Sprint ceremonies/meetings | Inherently human collaborative activity |
| Architecture decisions | Requires system-wide context AI doesn't have |
| Security audits | 45% of AI code has security flaws; AI review insufficient |
| Deployment/release decisions | Risk assessment requiring human judgment |
| Incident response/debugging | Root cause analysis in complex systems |
| Stakeholder communication | Interpersonal and context-dependent |
| Infrastructure management | Physical/operational constraints |
| Exploratory testing | Requires human intuition and domain knowledge |

### The "More Code = More Tests" Problem
- AI generates code 55% faster (GitHub RCT)
- But PRs are 18% larger, incidents per PR up 24%, change failure rates up 30%
- 59% of engineering leaders say AI code has errors at least half the time
- Code churn doubled from pre-AI baseline (GitClear, 211M lines analyzed)
- For every 25% increase in AI adoption, delivery stability drops 7.2% (DORA 2024)
- Testing/debugging already ~50% of conventional development time — making it the critical bottleneck

### The Estimation Multiplier
For non-code work estimation with AI-assisted development:
1. **Testing**: If AI accelerates coding by 50%, testing effort does NOT decrease proportionally — it may INCREASE due to more code, higher defect density, and unfamiliar generated code
2. **Code Review**: Review time increases as PR size grows 18% and AI code is "more taxing" to review
3. **Documentation**: Modest 10-15% time savings from AI drafting, but human revision required
4. **Deployment/Release**: Largely unchanged by AI; still requires human risk assessment
5. **Meetings/Ceremonies**: Completely unaffected by AI (~25% of sprint time)

---

## DATA QUALITY NOTES

### High-Confidence Sources (peer-reviewed or rigorous methodology)
- METR RCT (randomized controlled trial, published on arXiv)
- Google DORA 2024 Report (large-scale annual survey, established methodology)
- GitClear analysis (211M lines of code, quantitative analysis)
- ChatGPT test case generation study (arXiv, developer evaluation)
- GitHub Copilot RCT (arXiv:2302.06590)

### Medium-Confidence Sources (industry surveys, large sample but self-reported)
- IDC 2024 Report (established analyst firm, but behind paywall)
- Tidelift/Sonar Survey (reasonable methodology, self-reported)
- Stack Overflow 2024/2025 surveys (very large sample, self-reported)
- Rainforest QA 2025 Report (600+ respondents, but vendor-sponsored)

### Lower-Confidence Sources (industry claims, vendor marketing, blog analyses)
- CircleCI, Puppet, Forrester CI/CD claims (often cited without full methodology)
- "10x productivity" and "95% maintenance reduction" claims (vendor marketing)
- IBM error cost escalation figures (widely cited but original source unclear/old)
- Various Medium/LinkedIn analyses (individual perspectives, not primary research)

### Sources That Failed or Were Inaccessible
- WebFetch tool was denied permission for all direct page fetches
- Bash tool was denied permission for curl-based fetching
- IDC full report behind paywall (only summary data via InfoWorld)
- Several Medium articles available only via search result summaries
